raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.3,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.0,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should the improvement be limited to language and clarity (A: copyedit only) or include substantive revisions such as reordering sections and tightening claims to match evidence (B: structural and content-level edits)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "The methods narrative (detrending rationale, modeling steps, and conclusions) should read as a coherent, logically ordered argument.",
    "R1.3": "Performance and robustness statements (e.g., 89% agreement, cadence robustness) need to align tightly with what is actually reported in the text.",
    "R1.5": "A clearer section/subsection ordering of pipeline stages and benchmarking can substantially improve readability of a methods section.",
    "R1.6": "The draft contains long, compound sentences and repetitive phrasing that call for sentence-level clarity, grammar, and concision edits.",
    "RX.1": "The request is ambiguous between a light copyedit and a substantive revision, so uncertainty about scope should be surfaced.",
    "RX.2": "Edits should avoid amplifying claims beyond the reported results and should calibrate language around robustness appropriately.",
    "RX.3": "Work must stay within improving the provided text and not extend into designing new analyses or experiments.",
    "RX.4": "Avoiding misleading wording about methods and uncertainties reduces the risk of downstream misinterpretation in scientific contexts.",
    "RX.5": "Changes should be traceable to the original text so readers can verify what was modified and why."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Should the improvement be limited to language and clarity (A: copyedit only) or include substantive revisions such as reordering sections and tightening claims to match evidence (B: structural and content-level edits)?

rationale.R1.2: The methods narrative (detrending rationale, modeling steps, and conclusions) should read as a coherent, logically ordered argument.
rationale.R1.3: Performance and robustness statements (e.g., 89% agreement, cadence robustness) need to align tightly with what is actually reported in the text.
rationale.R1.5: A clearer section/subsection ordering of pipeline stages and benchmarking can substantially improve readability of a methods section.
rationale.R1.6: The draft contains long, compound sentences and repetitive phrasing that call for sentence-level clarity, grammar, and concision edits.
rationale.RX.1: The request is ambiguous between a light copyedit and a substantive revision, so uncertainty about scope should be surfaced.
rationale.RX.2: Edits should avoid amplifying claims beyond the reported results and should calibrate language around robustness appropriately.
rationale.RX.3: Work must stay within improving the provided text and not extend into designing new analyses or experiments.
rationale.RX.4: Avoiding misleading wording about methods and uncertainties reduces the risk of downstream misinterpretation in scientific contexts.
rationale.RX.5: Changes should be traceable to the original text so readers can verify what was modified and why.
